name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
طسٓمٓ (١)
```

```english
Ta, Seen, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
```

```english
These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (2)
```

```arabic
لَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
```

```english
You are liable to imperil your life [out of distress] that they will not have faith. (3)
```

```arabic
إِن نَّشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ءَايَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَـٰقُهُمْ لَهَا خَـٰضِعِينَ (٤)
```

```english
If We wish We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility. (4)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ (٥)
```

```english
There does not come to them any new reminder from the All-beneficent but that they disregard it. (5)
```

```arabic
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٦)
```

```english
They have already denied [the truth], but soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding. (6)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ (٧)
```

```english
Have they not regarded the earth, how many of every splendid kind [of vegetation] We have caused to grow in it? (7)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
```

```english
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (8)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of this Surah is to console the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the face of the denial he encountered from his people, who rejected the Book revealed to him by his Lord—as indicated by the opening of the Surah: **تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ** (These are the signs of the Manifest Book). They accused him at times of being possessed and at other times of being a poet. The Surah contains a warning to them, reinforced by recounting the stories of a group of Prophets: Musa, Ibrahim, Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, and Shu’ayb (عليه السلام), and the ultimate fate of those who denied them. This serves to comfort the soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) so that he may not grieve over the denial of the majority of his people, and so that the deniers may take heed.

The Surah is among the ancient Meccan Surahs and was among the first to be revealed. It contains the saying of the Exalted: **وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ** (And warn your nearest relations). It may be possible to infer—from the occurrence of this verse in this Surah and the occurrence of the saying: **فَاصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** (So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists) in Surah al-Hijr, and by comparing their contents—that this Surah was revealed earlier than Surah al-Hijr. The apparent context of the Surah's verses suggests they are all Meccan, though some have excepted the five verses at its end, and others have excepted the saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ آيَةً أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ عُلَمَاءُ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** (Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel know it?). Discussion regarding these will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **طسم تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ** — **Ta Seen Meem. These are the signs of the Manifest Book.** (26:1-2). The demonstrative "these" (تلك) refers to the verses of the Book that were to be revealed with the descent of the Surah and those revealed before it. The use of the distant demonstrative is to indicate their high status and exalted station. The term "Manifest" (المبين) is derived from *abana* (أبان), meaning it has become apparent and clear.

The meaning is: These verses, exalted in status and high in station, are the verses of the Book whose origin from Allah, the Glorified, is manifest and clear due to the mark of inimitability (الإعجاز) it bears, even if these stubborn polytheists deny it and label it at times as the promptings of the satans among the jinn and at other times as poetry.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَعَلَّكَ بَاخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ** — **You might kill yourself [with grief] because they will not believe.** (26:3). The term *al-bakhu’* (البخوع) refers to destroying oneself out of intense grief (وجد). The phrase "because they will not believe" (أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ) provides the reason for this self-destruction. The meaning is: It is expected of you that you might destroy yourself because of their lack of faith in the verses of this Book revealed to you.

The speech is framed as a rhetorical disapproval (الإنكار), and its purpose is to console the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَاقُهُمْ لَهَا خَاضِعِينَ** — **If We wish, We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility.** (26:4). The object of the "will" (المشية) is omitted because the recompense (الجزاء) indicates it. In the phrase "their heads will remain" (فظلت أعناقهم), *zalla* (ظل) is an incomplete verb (فعل ناقص) whose subject is "their necks" (أعناقهم) and whose predicate is "bowed in humility" (خاضعين). Humility is attributed to their necks—though it is a description of themselves—because humility first appears in a person's neck when they lower their head in submission; thus, it is a form of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي).

The meaning is: If We willed to send down upon them a sign that would humble them, compel them to accept, and force them into faith, We would send down such a sign, and they would remain clearly humbled by it, with their necks bowed.

It has been said that "necks" (الأعناق) refers to groups of people; others said it refers to their leaders and chiefs; and it was also said that a word is implied, meaning "the possessors of their necks remained humbled." This last view is the weakest of them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنَ الرَّحْمَنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ** — **There does not come to them any new reminder from the All-beneficent but that they disregard it.** (26:5). This is an explanation of their persistence in denying the signs of Allah and the deep-seated nature of their turning away from the remembrance of Allah in their souls, such that whenever a new reminder (ذكر) from the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) reaches them and they are called to it, they repel it by turning away.

The purpose is to describe their continuous turning away from every reminder that comes to them, not that they turn away from the "new" (محدث) reminder while accepting the "old" (قديم) one. The mention of the attribute "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) is an indication that the reminder that comes to them originates from the attribute of general mercy (الرحمة العامة) through which the rectification of their worldly and otherworldly affairs is achieved.

A discussion on the meaning of the "new reminder" (الذكر المحدث) was previously provided in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Anbiya; refer back to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنْبَاءُ مَا كَانُوا بِهِ يَيَسْتَهْزِئُونَ** — **They have already denied [it]; so soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding.** (26:6). This is a consequence (تفريع) of what preceded regarding their continuous turning away. The phrase "so soon there will come to them" (فسيأتيهم) is a further consequence. "News" (الأنباء) is the plural of *naba’* (نبأ), which refers to significant or grave news. The meaning is: Since they persisted in turning away from every reminder that came to them, it became a reality and was established against them that they have denied. And since their denial is established, the news of what they used to mock regarding the signs of Allah will come to them—this news being the immediate and deferred punishments that will encompass them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنْبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ** — **Have they not sighted the earth, how many of every generous kind We have caused to grow in it?** (26:7). The interrogative is for reproachful disapproval (الإنكار التوبيخي). The sentence is coordinated with an implied phrase indicated by the context, meaning: Did they persist in turning away and denying the signs, and yet they did not look at these generous pairs of plants that We caused to grow in the earth?

The "seeing" (الرؤية) in His saying "Have they not sighted" (أولم يروا) carries the meaning of "looking at" (النظر), which is why it is followed by the preposition *ila* (إلى). It appears that "generous pair" (الزوج الكريم)—which is said to mean "beautiful"—refers to the species of plants, as Allah, the Glorified, created their species in pairs. It has also been said that the "generous pair" which Allah caused to grow includes animals and specifically humans, based on the evidence of His saying: **وَاللَّهُ أَنْبَتَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ نَبَاتًا** (And Allah has caused you to grow from the earth like a plant).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ فِي ذَلِكَ لَآيَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** — **There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith.** (26:8). The demonstrative "that" (ذلك) refers to what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the growth of every generous pair, as it involves the creation of each pair and the completion of the deficiencies of each member of the pair by the other, guiding them toward the intended goal of their existence.

In this is a guidance for everything toward its ultimate felicity. For One whose sunnah (السنة) — *divine way or law* — is such, how could He neglect the affair of the human being, not guiding him to his felicity nor calling him to what contains the good of his worldly and otherworldly life? This is what the sign of the plants indicates.

His saying: "but most of them do not have faith" (وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ) means that it was not expected of the state of most of them—given their ingrained habit (ملكة) of turning away and the corruption of their innate capacity (الاستعداد)—that they would believe. The apparent meaning of the verse is similar to His saying: **فَمَا كَانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِمَا كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** (But they would not believe what they had denied before) (10:74). The attribution of disbelief and transgression to the deep-rooting of vile habits and the solidification of corruption in the inner self (السريرة) occurs in His speech more times than can be counted.

From this, it becomes clear that the statement of some—that the meaning is "it was not in the knowledge of Allah that they would believe"—is incorrect. In addition to being contrary to the immediate understanding of the sentence, there is no evidence that this is the intended meaning of the wording; rather, the evidence is to the contrary, as it was previously indicated that the habit of turning away was firmly rooted in their souls.

It is narrated from Sibawayh that "was" (كان) in His saying "but most of them do not have faith" (وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ) is a redundant connective (صلة زائدة), and the meaning is: "and most of them are not believers." While this is a correct meaning in itself, the context is more in line with the meaning previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** — **Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful.** (26:9). Because He, the Exalted, is the All-Mighty (العزيز) — *the Invincible who is never overcome* — He seizes those who turn away from His remembrance, deny His signs, and mock them, and He requites them with immediate and deferred punishments. And because He is the All-Merciful (الرحيم), He sends down the reminder to them to guide them, forgives those who believe in Him, and grants respite to the disbelievers.

## (A Philosophical Discussion Regarding Knowledge)

It is stated in *Ruh al-Ma’ani* regarding the saying of the Exalted: "but most of them do not have faith" (وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ): It was said: "meaning, it was not in the knowledge of Allah, the Exalted." This was objected to—on the basis that the context implies causality—that the knowledge of the Exalted is not the cause (العلة) for their lack of faith, because knowledge follows the known (المعلوم) and not vice versa.

This was countered by saying that the meaning of His knowledge being "follower" to the known is that His knowledge in pre-eternity (الأزل) of a specific originated known thing follows its quiddity (الماهية), in the sense that the specificity of the knowledge and its distinction from other knowledges is by virtue of it being knowledge of this quiddity. As for the existence of the quiddity in the temporal realm (فيما لا يزال), it follows the pre-eternal knowledge of the Exalted which follows its quiddity, in the sense that since He knew it in pre-eternity with this specificity, it must necessarily be realized and exist in the temporal realm accordingly. Thus, their very death upon disbelief and their lack of faith is the "followed" (متبوع) for His pre-eternal knowledge, and its occurrence is "follower" (تابع) to it. [End of quote].

This is an argument frequently found in the discourse of the proponents of determinism (المجبرة), especially Imam al-Razi in his *al-Tafsir al-Kabir*. They use it to prove determinism (الجبر) and negate free will (الاختيار). Its summary is that events—including human actions—are known to Allah, the Glorified, in pre-eternity; thus, their occurrence is necessary (ضرورية), otherwise His knowledge would be ignorance—exalted is He above that. Therefore, the human being is compelled (مجبر) to perform them and is not free (مختار). This was objected to by saying that knowledge follows the known and not vice versa, and the response was what he mentioned: that His knowledge in pre-eternity follows the quiddity of the known, but the known follows the knowledge in its existence.

This argument, in addition to the corruption of its premises in both foundation and structure, is a manifest fallacy (مغالطة). Firstly: The assumption of establishing something for the quiddity in pre-eternity and its existence in the temporal realm necessitates the precedence of quiddity over existence (الوجود). Where does quiddity get this fundamental reality (الأصالة) and precedence?

Secondly: The basis of the argument, as well as the objection and the response, rests on the assumption that His knowledge of things is acquired knowledge (العلم الحصولي) — *knowledge through mental concepts* — similar to our acquired knowledge related to concepts. Proof has been established in its proper place for the falsity of this, and that things are known to Him, the Exalted, through presential knowledge (العلم الحضوري) — *knowledge by presence where the known itself is present before the knower*. His knowledge is of two types: presential knowledge of things before their creation, which is identical to His Essence (الذات), and presential knowledge of them after their creation, which is identical to the existence of the things themselves. The detailed discussion is in its proper place.

Thirdly: Pre-eternal knowledge of its known in the temporal realm is only knowledge in the true sense of "knowledge" if it relates to it as it actually is—that is, with all its conditions, individuators, and existential specificities. Among the specificities of the existence of an action is that it consists of specific volitional, intentional movements (حركات إرادية اختيارية) proceeding from its specific agent, distinct from other involuntary movements (الحركات الاضطرارية) subsisting in his existence.

If this is the case, then the necessity (الضرورة) that attaches to the action by virtue of the knowledge relating to it is an attribute of the specific intentional action *as* a specific intentional action, not an attribute of the absolute action (الفعل المطلق), for the latter has no existence. That is, it was necessary that the action proceed from the will and choice of its agent; otherwise, the known would deviate from the knowledge. It is not that the knowledge relates to the intentional action and then strips the attribute of choice from its object and replaces it with the attribute of necessity and compulsion.

Thus, the argument has substituted the "absolute action" for the "specific action," considering it necessary, whereas what is necessary is the realization of the action characterized by choice—similar to the "contingent in itself" (الممكن بالذات) which is "necessary by another" (الواجب بالغير). Thus, the argument contains a fallacy by conflating the absolute action with the action conditioned by choice.

From here, it becomes clear that attributing the necessity of their lack of faith to the attachment of pre-eternal knowledge to it is unsound. The attachment of pre-eternal knowledge to an action only necessitates the necessity of its occurrence in the description it possesses: if it is intentional, its realization must be intentional; if it is unintentional, its realization must be so.

Furthermore, if the meaning of His saying: "but most of them do not have faith" (وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ) were the impossibility of their faith due to the attachment of pre-eternal knowledge to its absence, they would have taken it as an argument against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and considered it an excuse for themselves in their refusal to believe, as some of the proponents of determinism have admitted.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَاقُهُمْ لَهَا خاضِعِينَ** (If We wish, We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility): My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: **تخضع رقابهم يعني بني أمية ـ وهي الصيحة من السماء باسم صاحب الأمر** — "Their necks will be humbled—meaning the Umayyads—and it is the Cry from the sky with the name of the Master of the Affair (صاحب الأمر)."

**I say:** This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni in *Rawdat al-Kafi*, al-Saduq in *Kamal al-Din*, al-Mufid in *al-Irshad*, and al-Shaykh in *al-Ghaybah*. It appears that this is of the nature of "application" (الجري) — *the application of a verse to a later instance* — rather than "exegesis" (التفسير), as the context of the verse does not support it as the primary meaning.
